data.epi_content:
The city that once housed the Academy, where countless people gathered to form a massive metropolis and paid respects to the grand castle at its center, still had wisps of black smoke rising from it.

Humans who had starved to death, rotting remains, and motionless skeletons were still being discovered even after more than a month of burning.

Fortunately, at least the screams had stopped.

Loud shouts could still be heard, but they were cries of anger rather than terror—that was the only difference.

"If only you had come, you could have torn those damned black magicians limb from limb, and it still wouldn't have been enough."

A dark-skinned knight wearing a sword, covered in filth and still reeking of decomposing bodies, approached Vivian and raised his voice.

But Vivian didn't even glance at the knight, continuing to read her book while giving a half-hearted response.

"Yes, if I had gone, I might have been able to kill them both.

Though I'm certain one would have escaped."

"So, so you just agreed to let them go without even consulting anyone..."

"Yes. It's not important enough to discuss right now, so please leave."

The girl who once treated everyone kindly during her Academy days was gone, replaced by an imposing, bored-looking woman whose beauty remained unchanged. She closed her book and rubbed her eyes slightly.

Then she held her throbbing forehead and let out a sigh.

It was all too much of a headache for her.

"What should I have done?"

"You should have moved that heavy backside of yours and gone after those cursed black magician couple!

You're the only one who could have captured them, so what were you thinking when you just let them go..."

"Shut up. You know nothing, you fucking bla—no, just get out."

Vivian almost used the phrase "fucking blacks" that Erica often had on her lips, but she stopped herself and waved her hand, telling him to leave her room.

The knight let out a hollow laugh, crossed his arms, and leaned against the wall away from Vivian to catch his breath.

The heat that had risen to his head seemed to have subsided somewhat, and he began to speak more calmly.

"Hundreds of thousands of my people were killed by that cursed person's family.

I suppose you wouldn't care how many stupid blacks died."

Vivian's eyes twitched slightly as she watched the knight refer to himself as a "stupid black."

But it wasn't the time to show emotion. She had to let them go, even if she couldn't explain why.

"And now they've killed tens of thousands of people on this continent too, and you just, just let these murderers go...

If we had just known the reason, everyone wouldn't be this angry. Why did you let them go?"

Vivian didn't answer.

She didn't complain about her misfortune in facing monsters who would just come back to life even if killed.

It was an absurd, foolish story.

She didn't think anyone would believe her anyway.

"Lean. We simply compromised.

Erica and Evan will no longer...

They won't randomly kill people like they have been.

They won't destroy countries, execute lords, or sacrifice innocent people anymore."

"What if they don't keep their promise to you?
data.epi_content2:
Black magicians aren't likely to keep promises."

"Then I'll have to go and stop them somehow."

She couldn't bring herself to say she would kill them.

Vivian was no longer the type of person who could claim to do something she knew was impossible.

Meeting Evan and Erica had gradually twisted Vivian's once innocent and optimistic personality.

Like rubbing a clean white canvas against a dirty wall until it's covered in dust.

Her speech, personality, and thoughts had become so distorted that one could hardly believe she was the same student as before.

But something remained unchanged—her decisiveness, like when she had cast spells without hesitation upon seeing Erica and Evan boiling corpses.

"No one will accept this.

Even I, who have always believed in and followed you, cannot.

No one will forgive you."

At those words, Vivian's face contorted, and she began to get angry.

Just like the two black magicians.

In a way, it was deliberate anger.

By exploding with rage and intimidating the other person at this point, she could easily escape from difficult conversations.

She didn't want to resemble them, but through constant encounters, conversations, and exchanges of resentment, she was gradually becoming like them.

"So, who can force me to do anything?"

Vivian stood up, slammed her fist on the desk, and mockingly taunted the knight.

"The great Duke? The Marquis who was called the best until I made my name? Or you, who's useless except for swordsmanship?"

She carelessly tossed her book onto the sofa and, with practiced movements, took out a tea-scented cigarette from her pocket and put it in her mouth.

Even as she thought about how Erica had given her this bad habit, she justified to herself that nothing worked better for stress.

"Lean, answer me. Who can force me to do anything?"

"......"

"A mere black magician destroyed so many countries.

Fine, let's say those countries deserved to fall since they weren't in good shape to begin with.

Do you think I can't do the same?"

She picked up her staff and began to chant a spell.

Instantly, the room filled with a translucent energy.

"With a flick of my finger, I could turn you to dust right now.

Just by chanting a little spell..."

She stopped mid-sentence.

If she had continued, she might have actually done it.

"Remember my dedication.

I'm not asking for gratitude, just respect for what I've done so far."

After glaring at the knight for a long time, she sighed, dispelled the magic, and leaned back on the sofa, inhaling her tobacco deeply.

Although the smoke was surely harsh, her insides must have been burning even more intensely, as she didn't cough.
data.epi_content3:
"Get out."

The knight, with a slightly despairing or disappointed expression, walked out of the room.

Vivian stared at his back for a long time before breaking into a small laugh.

The laughter that came from nowhere seemed quite pitiful, but unfortunately, there was no man by her side to comfort her.

Even if there had been, she would have driven him away.

So, like most stressed people in power—who usually blow their brains out with a gun—she began to throw a hysterical fit, hurling everything in the room.

"What should I have done?! It's easy for you to talk!!

Do you think I had any other options?! How many times, how many times have I saved you all!!

You fools! Next time, that crazy Erica might kill all of us, including me!"

Only after shattering everything in the room—teacups with beautiful patterns, wooden boxes, bookshelves, portraits—did Vivian finally catch her breath.

She looked around at the room she had trashed for a long time, then grabbed her trembling legs and stood up.

With great difficulty.

The face that had been contorted with rage and laughter just moments ago was gone, replaced by an extremely cold, expressionless mask.

"No one knows. It doesn't matter."

She ran her hands over her face once, rubbing it dry for a long time.

Then she left the chaotic room behind and walked into the hallway.

"I'm going out for some fresh air. Please clean up the room."

Gone was her once kind tone, replaced by a curt manner as she ordered the servants to clean up. Vivian then dashed out, jumping through a window.

No one worried about her. She wasn't someone to worry about anyway.

She had long enjoyed the eccentricity of flying through the sky when feeling suffocated.

Everyone probably thought she was visiting the grave of her adoptive father, who had died two days ago when his wounds worsened.

...............

Vivian began wandering around the old Academy building after a long time.

The once splendid building was gone, its decorations and marble stripped away long ago, but traces of its existence remained, which wasn't a big problem.

Every building left in this land had been burned or destroyed long ago.

She walked for a long time through a path overgrown with weeds to a small pond and a half-broken wooden bench.

She gently stroked the bench, took out a cigarette from her pocket, lit it, and inhaled the smoke.

The pond was mostly destroyed, but it hadn't dried up because it was connected to the river that used to flow beside the Academy.

Then, a silly-looking carp caught her eye.

Unlike the flashy carp that swam in circles in confined spaces, this silly-looking one was freely swimming between the river and the half-destroyed pond.

Vivian stared at the carp for a long time before, displeased by something, she shot an icicle at it.

The carp quickly turned and fled toward the riverbank.

She hadn't wanted to kill it.

"Haha, ha. Shit."

She began to fill the air with smoke.

Instead of the joy and beauty that once filled her, it now carried anger and sorrow.
